Furl is doing the correct thing here, though it can be slightly confusing at
first glance.

The confusion boils down to how -- in the absense of URL delimiters (e.g. `://`,
`/`, `?`, etc) -- solitary strings like `www.example.com` are parsed.

While `www.example.com` looks like a host to you and me, how does furl know it's
a host? What about

```python
>>> furl('snakes')
```

Is `snakes` a host, too? Or is it a path? Remember host strings can be almost
anything -- they don't need to contain periods (e.g. `localhost`). That, and
every valid host string is also a valid path segment.
`http://foo.com/url/www.example.com/` is a perfectly valid URL, too.

So, to be consistent, furl parses all single-component strings (strings without
multiple URL components separated by URL delimiters) as path strings. You can
see this behavior with `www.example.com` above.

```python
>>> f = furl('www.example.com')
>>> f.host
None
>>> f.path.segments
['www.example.com']
```

This is also exactly the behavior of Python's [urlparse](https://docs.python.org/2/library/urlparse.html) module, too.

```python
>>> from urlparse import urlparse
>>> urlparse('www.example.com')
ParseResult(scheme='', netloc='', path='www.example.com', params='', query='', fragment='')
```

Back to your original example. There are three slashes (`///`) in the final URL
because `www.example.com` is parsed as a path segment and not a host.

```python
>>> f = furl('www.example.com').set(scheme='http').add(path='test/path')
>>> f.path.segments
['www.example.com', 'test', 'path']
>>> f.host
None
```

If you want furl to adopt `www.example.com` as the host, explicitly set it as
the host with `set()`. Like

```python
>>> f = furl().set(host='www.example.com', scheme='http').add(path='test/path')
>>> f.path.segments
['test', 'path']
>>> f.host
'www.example.com'
```

I've thought about adding the ability for furl to 'guess' whether a solitary
string is the host string or a path segment based on whether or not that string
contains periods, but that would lead to inconsistent behavior. Consistent
behavior, even if sometimes initially surprising, is better behavior.

SAM/CRAM do not have the same limitation. 64k cigar is a BAM only issue. You can convert SAM to BAM as follows:
```sh
git clone https://github.com/lh3/htsbox
cd htsbox; make
./htsbox samview -bS in.sam > out.bam
```
Sorting and viewing `out.bam` with old tools will preserve reads with long cigars. However, old pileup and igv will not see them.

I also thought about an `else` statement there, but I think the callback of a subscribed channel is already called prior to that:

In `backends/bgapi.py` we read bytes from serial in `_receive`. 
This populates the `_receiver_queue` (both with notifications and responses to char read), that is also used in `expect_any`. 
But prior to inserting it in the queue, in `_receive` we also call `receive_notification` of the connected device, which in turn calls the registered callbacks for a subscribed channel.
So I think calling the callback in `expect_any` would be redundant.

It looks like the user you're running zap-cli as doesn't have write permissions in the directory `/usr/local/zaproxy`. `zap-cli` puts the log output for ZAP into a file called `zap.log` in that directory, so you need to be able to write to that directory. A simple fix might be to just take ownership of that directory, assuming you're running `zap-cli` as the user `ifs`:
```
sudo chown -R ifs.ifs /usr/local/zaproxy
```

Then it should hopefully work. :)

I think this is great. At the moment, it returns `lower_bound = upper_bound = longitudes[0]`, which means the gap between `lower_bound` and `upper_bound` is 0. This causes the `merge_longitude_bounds` function to fail (it assumes that lower_bound is always less than upper bound, up to a normalisation constant). Note that this problem predates your fix -- it was just masked by it.

It would seem reasonable to expect a `longitude_bounds` function to always return bounds that are strictly ordered (i.e. `lower_bound < upper_bound`). One way to do this would be:

```python
def longitude_bounds(longitudes):
    N = float(len(longitudes))
    if N > 1:
        # code for N > 1
    else:
        lower_bound = upper_bound = longitudes[0]
    lower_bound, upper_bound = _ensure_gap_in_bounds(lower_bound, upper_bound)
    return lower_bound, upper_bound

def _ensure_gap_in_bounds(lower_bound, upper_bound):
    if abs(upper_bound - lower_bound) < 2.0*EPSILON:
        lower_bound -= EPSILON
        upper_bound += EPSILON
    return lower_bound, upper_bound
```

For consistency, we could do the same in the `latitude_bounds` function.

Besides this, I have two suggestions:

 - Do you think it's more readable to have the special case first:

```python
if N == 1:
    lower_bound = upper_bound = longitudes[0]
else:
    # rest 
return lower_bound, upper_bound
```

- Should we duplicate the unit test for latitude bounds?

@avilchess @mquinson

The proposed fixes here should rectify the problems Exhale has with your project's function overloads.

If you have time would you be willing to quickly install and run this branch?  Function overloads ended up being much more complicated than I anticipated.

```console
$ pip install git+http://github.com/svenevs/exhale.git@fix/function-overloads#egg=exhale
```

I would like to release a new version with the fixes in this branch.  I can verify that the codebase for #13 succeeds with these fixes (finally...), but it would be really helpful to get confirmation / feedback from users if you have time :)

1. Functions in the full api listing include their fully qualified name.  So `namespace foo { void bar(); }` used to have its title as `Function bar` and now is `Function foo::bar`.
    - Would you rather this not be the case?  Would you rather it be configurable via yet-another-config-variable?
    - Motivation: "flat" naming is not very useful in the main Library API page, which is where all of the functions get enumerated (they aren't in any hierarchies).

2. When a function overload is detected, its parameters are included in the title of the function page.

    ```cpp
    void bar(int);         // => Function bar(int)
    void bar(float, float);// => Function bar(float, float)
    void foo(int);         // => Function foo ---> No parameters when no overload
    ```
3. Warnings will be emitted for overload groups (group := function name) that cannot currently be documented correctly.  As far as I can tell, Breathe currently only accepts `func_name(list, of, parameters)`.  When templates are different but function parameters are the same, I cannot get Breathe to do this.  So these

    https://github.com/svenevs/exhale/blob/0a0ae56c29ebc7c8e838ffa8ce5a81d303aa6146/testing/projects/cpp_func_overloads/include/overload/overload.hpp#L79-L99

    All produce `.. doxygenfunction:: overload::bar(typename C::type)`, which will lead to it documented three times on the Library API page, also resulting in warnings about multiple declarations from Sphinx.  Exhale produces a very umm noticable warning about this and proposes temporary alternatives (particularly how to skip with Doxygen).

Thank you for any thoughts / suggestions / feedback!

@heitzmann I would like to add another piece of info. In order for us to overcome the above errors, we introduce a minute inclination in what should have been perfectly horizontal waveguides. This breaks the symmetry and allows gdspy to execute without errors.

So, this is what happens:

1. autopep8 reindents all lines globally (https://github.com/hhatto/autopep8/blob/159bb88843e298534e46914da242e680a1c8c47d/autopep8.py#L3322)
2. running pycodestyle after that doesn't reveal any W191 warnings
3. since autopep8 doesn't know anything about W191, you can't ignore it

One solution is to prevent autopep8 from reindenting all lines globally:
```
$ autopep8.py test.py --ignore=E101,E111 -v
--->  Applying global fix for E265
--->  Applying global fix for W602
--->  5 issue(s) to fix {'W191': set([4, 5]), 'E302': set([3]), 'E225': set([1, 5])}
--->  2 issue(s) to fix {'W191': set([5, 6])}
a = 2


def someFn():
        print('hello there')
        print(a == 3)
```

Ideally `--ignore=E101` should've been enough, but I think this piece of code is broken: https://github.com/hhatto/autopep8/blob/159bb88843e298534e46914da242e680a1c8c47d/autopep8.py#L3320, therefore you need both `-ignore=E101,E111`

both reading from and writing to the database _must_ happen in the ‘parent’ process.

`itertools.groupby()` returns special ‘grouper’ objects, which are wrappers around the underlying iterators, which in plyvel's case would be leveldb iterators which cannot be shared between processes.

you may consume each group into an actual list though:

```
it = db.iter(...)
it = itertools.groupby(it, ...)
it = (list(group) for group in it)

pool.map(f, it)
```

Hi

Yes, this is expected behavior. `on_press_key` (and similar `_key` functions) looks for key labels regardless of modifiers. I understand this can be confusing, but it's the logical conclusion I had to take.

There are several types of keys that share names, or that have their names modified:

- Letters become uppercase when shift is pressed, or caps lock is on. `on_press_key('A')` should match even an unshifted `a`, and `on_press_key('a')` should not stop working when shift is held or caps lock is on.
- Therefore, `on_press_key('1')` should also be equivalent to `on_press_key('!`)` (modulo keyboard layout).
- Num lock is a modifier, so pressing `num 1` should trigger a hook for `end` (but not vice-versa!).

I don't know of any other way to make this less confusing without breaking consistency.

The standard way to create a topic is to enable topic auto creation in your server properties (consult server documentation on this), then submit a MetadataRequest for the new topic name. The server will then begin initializing the new topic, electing a leader, and starting replication as configured. This process does take a short amount of time, so it is quite normal to see a small lag between when the server first "learns" of the new topic name and when it will accept produce and fetch requests.

Dearest @Stonesjtu, this is precisely what the `foce_styling=True` argument to blessings.Terminal() was designed to support, and blessings supports this use case fine. http://blessings.readthedocs.io/en/latest/#blessings.Terminal.__init__

ls(1) would have this same problem, and must be forced -- `watch --color 'ls -FG'` does not work on my mac with gnu watch 3.3.12 installed, however, from manual of ls(1) for mac, `CLICOLOR_FORCE=1 watch --color 'ls -FG'` causes color output to work, for the very same reason of blessings, that color is not used when not connected to a terminal.

example:

```
watch --color 'TERM=ansi python -c "import blessings;print(blessings.Terminal(force_styling=True).blue(\"smurf\"))"'
```

note that this "color interpretation" by watch is very poor and may not support most of the sequences that blessings can generate, and depends on a number of things about your system and current `TERM` type, here we set `TERM=ansi` to help stay within watch(1)'s strict ansi decoder, which can be seen here: https://gitlab.com/procps-ng/procps/blob/4e2a1ba9b194f359471f80bd4d00e591ba740ce4/watch.c#L141

When color is necessary, I often just do:
```
while [ 0 ]; do ./XXX.py; sleep 1; clear; done
```

(The only good use for watch(1) instead of simple shell script is when the `-d` diff argument is needed, perhaps. this is the only feature watch(1) offers that cannot be rapidly scripted in any shell!)

First: Thanks for using and liking my package, and taking the time to create a PR! 

I think that this functionality shouldn't be part of `fixedwidth`, for these reasons:

* It alters the data (via rounding), which I don't think it should do, and others may not expect. Especially since fixed-width data (in my experience) is usually going to a bank
* Formatting (such as removing decimal points from numbers) seems like it's outside the scope of this package -- this should be handled in the program which imports this package
* Decimals are already supported for most use cases
* The amount of code added (increases the codebase by more than 50%) increases the complexity and readability more than I think the feature is worth -- especially since most people don't need the feature

However, I'm going to leave this PR open for a while in case anyone has arguments that may change my mind. Also, I hope you have no trouble refactoring the changes to use in another part of the data chain. It's usually a good idea to propose changes before putting the work in so the effort isn't wasted if the feature is rejected, but in this case I think you should be able to re-use the code pretty easily.

That's very helpful info. I could process the scans setting model=1999. What that does in the program is dictate how the data from SON files are read in. Specifically, it means there are 68 rather than 67 header bytes per data packet. It also changes some aspects of the byte-ordering of data written to each header packet, which makes it difficult to parse out heading and location info. It is probable that it is the firmware, not the model, which dictates this. In that regard, analyzing your data was very enlightening. I would need to modify the program to automatically 'catch' this, which apparently in what SonarTRX is doing. But, the long-and-short of it is, model=1999 works for you.

The other issue of heading is probably related to you using an external GPS. I don't know what convention that uses, but it possible it is different from a course-over-ground north-east-up convention expected by PyHum. Therefore, using the flag cog=0 in the read module might be the solution. I will test this tomorrow when I'm back in the office

Hi monksp -

Thanks so much for this P.R. - Here are my thoughts:

The context local variables are meant to be programmer-friendly representations of their corresponding ASK abstractions.

If you look back at core.py, you'll see two methods `_parse_request` and `_parse_session` which try to do safe attribute copying of the json into internal Flask-Ask structures (i.e. _Request, _Session, _Intent). There's also some some timestamp conversion in `_parse_request`. This is to make it so the programmer doesn't have to do those things and can work with the result using dot notation instead of dictionary access. i.e. 

```
request.timestamp  # datetime.dateime
```

vs.

```
request['timestamp']  # str
```

Therefore, the context variable should follow the same convention.

Yeah, the unicity of nameplates is important, and probably more challenging to achieve with a distributed allocation process. It's easy to make them unique by making them long and random, but they have to be transcribed from one client to the other (through the humans), so we can't afford to have them be very long.

Partitions in the DHT will cause problems: clients on either side of the partition won't be able to talk to each other. And it won't be obvious that you're split this way: from Alice's point of view, Bob is just taking a really long time to connect. I'd imagine that most of those DHTs are pretty robust against problems like that, though, so maybe it's not really much of an issue.

I'm especially interested in looking at how IPFS does things, because I think "bootstrap realtime communication through the DHT" is part of their design goals. I think their DHT-ish layer has clients make successive connections through the leaves until they find the original publishing node, and then make a direct connection there (the DHT is also helping with the NAT hole-punching task). Our mailbox/rendezvous server has two goals: deliver messages quickly between simultaneously-connected clients, and queue messages reliably for non-simultaneously-connected clients. Maybe the IPFS-style approach would work for the first case, and if you can't find the target node online, then you queue the messages in some neighboring node for them to find when they connect again later.

It's fine! Usually, if we want to edit commits, we use `git rebase`. You can use it to squash (fuse two or more commits together), reorganize, and reword your previous commits. If you want to modify the commit message, you should look for the `reword` command in `git rebase`.

Be careful though, and make sure that every time you commit, you are just editing one file (or subset of files). Rebase commands can cause conflicts within the history etc. But if you're just rewording your commit messages, then there's no problem.

Some helpful links:
- https://help.github.com/articles/changing-a-commit-message/
- https://git-scm.com/book/en/v2/Git-Tools-Rewriting-History

In practice, what I usually do is to have `[WIP]` (Work In Progress) commits. So my commit messages before rebasing looks like this:

```
[WIP] Update files
[WIP] Add this method to file
[WIP] Add two more methods on same file
[WIP-test] Add test cases
[WIP-test] More tests
```

This categorizes commits related to the main file and the test files. So after rebasing, it will now look like this:

```
Add file for doing stuff
Add test cases for file that does stuff
```

But of course, your mileage may vary, check what works for you :+1:

This is due to a limitation in the YAML protocol which only supports one unique entry per key, but it's easily bypassable by replace, this:

```yaml
    IdentityFile: /Users/1/.ssh/1/id_rsa
    IdentityFile: /Users/2/.ssh/2/id_rsa
    IdentityFile: /Users/3/.ssh/3/id_rsa
```

to this:

```yaml
    IdentityFile:
      - /Users/1/.ssh/1/id_rsa
      - /Users/2/.ssh/2/id_rsa
      - /Users/3/.ssh/3/id_rsa
```

---

Having a table of 128 float columns (`f1`, `f2`, ..., `f128`), you can build composite index on several of them (e.g. `(f1, f2, f3, f4)`) for selection optimization, and then query
```sql
SELECT id, POW(f1 - :e1) + POW(f2 - :e2) + ... + POW(f128 - :e128) AS square_distance FROM encodings WHERE f1 > :minF1 AND f1 < :maxF1 AND f2 > :minF2 AND f2 < :maxF2 ... AND f128 > :minF128 AND f128 < :maxF128
ORDER BY square_distance ASC LIMIT 1
```
where 
```
:eX = encodingX
:minFX = encodingX - 0.1 * abs(encodingX)
:maxFX =  encodingX + 0.1 * abs(encodingX)
```
`0.1` can vary from 0 to 1, to define how strict is selection.

This should bring you a row with minimal vector distance to searched encodings.
It may also return nothing if the selection is too strict.

Maybe your family members look similar, so using a more strict tolerance will make the model more sensitive. Try passing a lower value for tolerance to compare_faces, like this:

```python
results = face_recognition.compare_faces(known_faces, unknown_face_encoding, tolerance=0.5)
```

You can use the `face_recognition.face_distance()` function to check the face distance between your family members to find the best tolerance to use.

One of my parameter bound was inverted (*duh...*). I basically provided:`{'x1': (x1_max, x1_min)}`.
This issue is basically a dup of [Issue 41](https://github.com/fmfn/BayesianOptimization/issues/41) and can be closed as 'user error'. 

To answer your questions:

 1. I am not entirely sure how `res.fun` got to be scalar exactly but providing 'inverted' min and max bounds for a parameter certainly seems to trigger the issue.
 2. The rationale behind `.min()`was: if `res.fun` can be either a scalar or an array, then we taking the minimum of `res.fun` would always give us what we want (the min of `-res.fun` would be the max of `Y`.

Thanks again!

The empty body for the LoginView is so that another auth method is used when obtaining the token - knox intends for another auth class to be used (eg: BasicAuthentication) for the request to provide an initial authentication, and then use the generated token for the rest of the session. This means that one can plug in any authentication method as applicable (eg: OAuth, Digest, or some custom method).

An empty body is the most auth-method-agnostic way of dealing with that, AFAIK :smile:

On Sun, Jan 31, 2016 at 2:29 AM, Andrew <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> @percyliang <https://github.com/percyliang>
> Now all frontend vendor dependencies are tracked via Bower. This
> creates two new directories: static/bower_components for all vendor
> dependencies that used to live in static/js/vendor, as well as a
> static/custom directory for all custom modified JS/CSS, eg. our
> custom Modernizr.
>
> There are a lot of files because I'm checking in the bower_components
> <https://addyosmani.com/blog/checking-in-front-end-dependencies/>
> (considered best practice).
>
> Most important changes:
>
>    -
>
>    Added new npm script for building:
>
>    npm run bower # gathers all bower dependencies.
>    -
>
>    Removed the static/vendor directory, replacing it instead with
>    static/bower_components and static/custom, the latter being for things
>    like custom.modernizr that we can't pull directly from another source.
>    -
>
>    Downside: checking in the bower components take up 233MB in the
>    repository :( I'm not sure if there's a great way around this. There are
>    only two choices to deal with this. a) Don't check in the bower_components
>    or b) check them in and just ignore it. The problem with (b) is that we
>    just made our repo a ton larger, and there's added slowdown when doing a
>    clone/checkout. Problem with (a) is that we won't be able to fetch the
>    bower_components for deployment if GitHub is down (as that is where Bower
>    downloads all components from. At jobs I've run into problems where the
>    external IP of the company had exceeded the Github rate limit, so no one
>    could do a bower install for several hours. Don't want this to happen when
>    trying to deploy...)

Unfortunately it's not easy to fix this from from bcbio since the scheduler is killing the parent job (`run_bcbio.sh`) that controls everything. Essentially we've handed over control to the scheduler and if it cuts off the parent process no more jobs will get dispatched. To fix, we'd have to be able to intercept or identify the scheduler's kill command and then trigger removal of jobs before dying. I'm not sure how to do this on any scheduler well, much less reliably across multiple ones. This might be a case where it would make sense to set higher limits for your main job to account for the long wait times in your queue. Hope this helps some.

Hello,
You seem to have an old version of the currency converter. Note that the data is embedded within the package, to avoid making expensive HTTP calls to an external service when requesting every single rate. So, an old package means old data. To always use up to date data, you have two solutions:

* update your currency converter version, either from PyPI, or from GitHub (updated more often)
* at initialization time, you can instantiate the converter like this (documented at the end of the [README](https://github.com/alexprengere/currencyconverter/#other-attributes)):
  ```python
  from currency_converter import CurrencyConverter
  c = CurrencyConverter('http://www.ecb.int/stats/eurofxref/eurofxref-hist.zip')
  ```
  This way, the latest data will be fetched when creating the `c` object, you just have to have access to the internet at initialization time.

In the interface under "Additional Files" there is a section where you can add files that are not python scripts. This is meant for things like images, data-sets and things like your .ui files. 
You can't expect for pyinstaller to copy all the files that you call (like GP.ui) as a lot of the time they are created a runtime, so it is up to you to simply add them to the package using an extra command. This extra command has been implemented into the interface as a simple file reference field.
If you want to import GP.ui from the base directory, I recommend changing "assets/" to ".".

The problem, though, is the database model only stores a phone number and not a country code.

I think the `phonenumbers` package has methods to figure out a country based on just the phone number, but it's rather intensive on memory and has to sort through XML files.

If you really want this type of field, it'd probably be best to look at the source code here and make your own similar one that had 2-letter country iso codes instead of international dialing codes and then compile the phone number yourself with `phonenumbers` and store the number along with the country code in the database.

So, it turns out the problem is with (dependencies of) `lessc`. In particular, [this line](https://github.com/less/less.js/blob/master/bin/lessc#L356) requires the `mkdirp` package, and otherwise falls back to `mkdirSync`, as is seen in my stacktrace. By installing `mkdirp` (e.g. `node-mkdirp` via `apt`), it is now able to create full paths, and everything is golden.

Per SQLite.org -- there is no "truncate" command; most other SQL varieties, including Postgres, MySQL, MSSQL do have "truncate" 

 [SQLite Delete vs Truncate ](https://www.sqlite.org/lang_delete.html) says--- 
The Truncate Optimization

When the WHERE is omitted from a DELETE statement and the table being deleted has no triggers, SQLite uses an optimization to erase the entire table content without having to visit each row of the table individually. This "truncate" optimization makes the delete run much faster. Prior to SQLite version 3.6.5 (2008-11-12), the truncate optimization also meant that the sqlite3_changes() and sqlite3_total_changes() interfaces and the count_changes pragma will not actually return the number of deleted rows. That problem has been fixed as of version 3.6.5 (2008-11-12).

The truncate optimization can be permanently disabled for all queries by recompiling SQLite with the SQLITE_OMIT_TRUNCATE_OPTIMIZATION compile-time switch.

The truncate optimization can also be disabled at runtime using the sqlite3_set_authorizer() interface. If an authorizer callback returns SQLITE_IGNORE for an SQLITE_DELETE action code, then the DELETE operation will proceed but the truncate optimization will be bypassed and rows will be deleted one by one.

The name is available during class-construction, however this is only true as long as the trait and observe method are defined on the same class. This means you'd still want a string based api when subclassing, and personally I think it'd be better to stick to a single api, rather than supporting many.

As far as `@property` goes, the reason you need to maintain the same name across functions is because the `getter` and `setter` wrapper methods of a property actually return a new property instance. Thus forcing users to keep everything about the property confined to a single name. In our case though, because of the way the descriptors are initialized, defining traits, observers, and validators under different names is really the only way to go.

@butla Some databases like MySQL or Postgre still accept `\'` to escape the single quote. Therefore we can perform an injection with the following:

```
>>> unsafe = "John\\'; DROP TABLE the_table --"
>>> print(unsafe)
John\'; DROP TABLE the_table --
>>> q = Query.from_(t).select('*').where(t.name == unsafe)
>>> q
SELECT * FROM "the_table" WHERE "name"='John\''; DROP TABLE the_table --'
```

The term `'John\''` will be understood by vulnerable databases as a literal for `John'`. Note that the final quote is ignored because of a comment terminating the statement.

Additionally some database may use pre-defined conversions from Unicode to ASCII, e.g. converting `‘` to `'`, which wouldn't be spotted by pypika.

The problem here is that pypika is just the tool that produces the query, and the robustness of a query against SQL injection is highly dependent on how the query will be handled by the database, which pypika is totally decoupled from. Parametrized queries are an interface exposed by databases themselves, therefore ensuring their security. It's just that expect not only a string but also a tuple (of values) as input, making pypika not compatible with such interface.

Regarding the changes in the hashes, I am thinking about this:

1. Do not change `Pipenv`
2. Remove `Pipfile.lock`
3. Run `pipenv --lock`

Now I will eventually have changes in `Pipfile.lock` even though the package versions have not been changed since, as you said above "it is totally fine for package maintainers to upload new files to a version after it is published". If I check the changes in the (committed) `Pipfile.lock` I will eventually see hash changes. 

This makes `Pipfile.lock` poorly suited to ensure that my application is built from a reproducible set of packages.

Or said, differently, `pipenv --lock` is not an idempotent operation: the same input `Pipfile` can produce a different output, even with fully pinned versions for all packages, since the ultimate source (`PyPi` for example), can change.

I do not mean this in an academic sense: this is what I am seeing very often when doing `pipenv --lock`

I see what's going on here, it's prioritizing the resolution of the VCS dependencies (we don't typically recommend listing non-top-level dependencies like django unless it's absolutely necessary) -- in your case I think you should take these out of editable mode -- I can see that the second dependency needs `django-floppyforms` and `django-talkback` so you should put those in your Pipfile to make sure they wind up in your lockfile also.

Basically this will stop the resolver from attempting to find the dependencies of the git-referenced packages and putting them in the lockfile.

The other option is to just put these in `dev-packages` and the version pinned in `packaages` should be preferred

@tsiq-oliverc Since the best practice of app requires you to pin your dependencies, libraries would start to pin theirs as well, if they use the same source of requirement files. This would lead to problems in dependency resolution.

Say my app has two dependencies A and B, both of them depend on C, but A pins v1, while B pins v2. Compiled languages allow the toolchain to detect this at compile time, and resolve it in many ways. Rust, for example, does this during linking time—The end executable would contain two copies of C (v1 and v2), with A and B linking to each of them. In C++ land this would be solved with dynamic libraries; the symbol lookup is done even later (at runtime), but the idea is the same—the compiler knows what you need (from the interface you use), and can act accordingly.

Scripting languages can’t do this because it doesn’t know what you really want to do until it actually reaches the call. Node works around this by *always* assuming the dependencies are incompatible (A and B always get their own C, even if the two copies are identical), but that leads to a new class of problems, and results in awkward hacks like peer dependencies that everyone (I hope?) agrees are terrible. Python probably don’t want to go there (it can’t, anyway, since that would likely break all existing Python installations).

Another way to work around this is to do something clever in the packaging tools that “unpins” the dependency version. Bundler (of Ruby) sort of does this, by recommending people to not include the lock file into the gem, so Bundler can use the unpinned versions in Gemfile, instead of pinned versions in Gemfile.lock. But people tend to ignore advices and do whatever they want, so you still get pinned versions everywhere.

I don’t think this can be done, personally, and I’d continue to think this way until I see the very brilliant proposal that actually does it.

@silkentrance I know how to write the excludes, what I'm saying is that currently `find_packages()` just works for a large fraction of all Python projects. Most Python projects are *not* using namespaces and the `src` layout is not as popular as the "package directly in root".

What that means is that by changing the *default* thing that `find_packages` does, for *most* people we're making `find_packages` *harder* to use, not easier. What's more, if you continued to use `find_packages` as the majority of people do - which is to say using `find_packages()` or `find_packages(excludes=['tests'])` or some limited thing, when someone starts using `python setup.py` with a recent version of `setuptools`, it will silently start including a bunch of directories like `build` and `test`. A lot of them won't even notice this is happening.

Changing the default for `find_packages` would cause a bunch of headaches and break a bunch of existing code for very little benefit. On the other hand, we could *easily* make this behavior opt-in, so that anyone with a namespace package can just do:

```python
PACKAGES = find_packages(include=['src'], implicit_namespace=True)
```

And it will work just fine. If it's so easy for people to manually include and exclude their package directories, why is it so difficult to ask people with implicit namespace packages to opt-in to this behavior? After all, you want the default option to be the *common case* and the opt-in to be the *uncommon case*. Implicit namespace packages are decidedly the uncommon case.

> In CRF learning, previous word's label can be used for features like f_k(y_i-1, y_i, x_i). How can I add such features related to labels to the Trainer? Also, how can I add such features to inputs of Tagger.tag()? If Tagger.tag() only received features already determined as numbers (or boolean), it cannot express features related to labels in my understanding.

CRFSuite doesn't support arbitrary `f_k(y_i-1, y_i, x_i)` features; it implements two kinds of features: `I(y_i=a)*f(xseq)` (called "state features") and `I(y_i=a)*I(y_i-1=b)` ("transition features"). `f(xseq)` is what you pass to tagger/trainer; `I(y_i=a)*f(xseq)` and `I(y_i=a)*I(y_i-1=b)` are auto-generated for all possible labels `a` and `b`. 

It is not possible to condition transition probability on input in CRFsuite, i.e. `I(y_i=a)*I(y_i-1=b)*f(xseq)` features are not implemented. If you need them then you'll have to use another crf package, e.g. wapiti (and python-wapiti).

To the best of my knowledge, you're experiencing expected behavior of typical TCP based networks, especially when it comes to wireless networks. Networks are unpredictable by nature and it's difficult for OS network layers to know exactly why a connection is no longer communicating. The OS cannot prematurely assume the connection has been severed. Most TCP stacks will maintain the "connection" until a timeout is passed (usually set by the OS and are extremely long). Hence why no errors or callbacks triggers in your case, you basically haven't hit those timeout thresholds yet and the OS still thinks you're connected despite clearly being disconnected. In theory, this should allow the other end enough time to respond, fix the connection, send packets, etc.

To fix your issue, I think you'll have to use the `deliverBody` function for `Response` objects.

``` python
from twisted.internet import defer, protocol, task
import treq

class CustomConsumer(protocol.Protocol):
    def __init__(self, finished):
        self.finished = finished

    def dataReceived(self, data):
        print(data)
        # using something like callLater you can check flags to ensure file is still downloading

    def connectionLost(self, reason):
        self.finished.callback('[x] finished consuming')

def consume_content(response, deferred):
    response.deliverBody(CustomConsumer(deferred))
    return deferred

@task.react
def main(reactor):
    finished = defer.Deferred()
    finished.addCallback(print)

    request = treq.get('http://localhost:9000')
    request.addCallback(consume_content, finished)
    return finished
```

Vote to close issue since this particular issue but another one should be opened to document `deliverBody` usage in reference to treq. I don't think most will know to look at twisted docs for this stuff.

The big problem with python3 porting is that ldaptor uses the `__str__` method of objects as the wire format for an object. This complicates the code as str() is bytes in python2 while on python3 this is text/unicode.

I suggest to stop using str() for wire format, and create a dedicate method ( toWire() for example) which will always return bytes and used before sending data to the transport.

We can keep str() as is now on Python2 for backward compatibility.

I didn't tested this, but it sounds like not specifying `n_to_return` in GETMORE makes it to behave like initial QUERY response -- return `min(101pcs, 1Mb)` of documents. But QUERY seems to always return `min(101pcs, 1Mb)` of data, regardless of what we specify in `n_to_return`.

This is suboptimal if you are expecting to get thousands of docs from DB, but this is how MongoDB behaves when you don't specify `batchSize`. This is probably a kind of trade-off between initial response time and bulk performance. The proper way to get maximum performance when fetching large datasets is to specify `batchSize` to large number. But unfortunately, txmongo doesn't (yet) support this.

The algorithm looks very interesting, I think I will try it on my own images at some point (it would need to be extended to 3-D images, though -- but it shouldn't be too difficult I think?). However, if you're interested, I can suggest that we keep on reviewing your PR until it is "scikit-image compatible", so that if the algorithm grows popular we can merge it in a while. What do you think?

I haven't reviewed the PR in detail, but one thing that I noticed is that you use object-oriented programming, with one class for the algorithm. This programming workflow is usually very convenient at the beginning, but it is less modular on the long run. For example, in the ``random_walker_segmentation.py`` module, the algorithm is coded with functions only, so that it would be possible to re-use some of these functions in other modules, if necessary (like the function to build the Laplacian, as I suggested in a comment). It is less convenient if you don't have functions, but methods of a class. Even for your future research projects it might be useful for you to be able to re-use some functions (which lowers the probability of bugs ;-)).

So, even if it involves passing parameters (which shouldn't have any impact on performance) from one function to the other, I'd recommend that you write the algorithm using functions instead of a class, for the sake of modularity and maintainability. What do you think?

Yes, that works. But users won't expect this behavior.

> The main problem with the current bibclassify is that it needs a thesaurus. 

Perhaps we could support a simple "controlled vocabulary", but in practice I think this would probably quickly lead to working with full-scale ontologies.  Soon after starting with a controlled vocabulary, people would probabyl ask "How do I define a synonym for my controlled vocabulary term?" or "How do I defined an alternative term for this expression?".  Perhaps we could just facilitate initial ontology building, e.g. offer populating it from the list of frequent keywords that papers already have.  Just an example.

> If bibclassify could have like a global view of a repository it would be a big plus. For example if I have two universities in medical area, it would be efficient to determine most used keywords to try to define their main field of competencies.

Considering that the system contains papers published by the researchers of these two universities, and considering that each paper has a list of keywords, then asking for the field of competency of each university is just another "search summary page", like the author pages.  So the "global view of the repository" use case is just an extension of search, as it were, simply building upon the powers of BibClassify, that should properly populate the papers' keywords with proper terms in the first place.

We currently have two attributes for identifying a user (if they decide to login via the normal login form):

This issue is a problem of django-rest-framework-jwt, I think. When user which is not activated try to login, Django refuse by default（`'django.contrib.auth.backends.ModelBackend'`）. This is because of **'The user is not activated.'**, not **'Unable to log in with provided credentials.'**.  
Current implemention of django-rest-framework-jw try to handle this, but it contains a bug , so it return **'Unable to log in with provided credentials.'**.

The `tests/system.py` module computes `EXISTING_INSTANCES` at the beginning of the test run, and expects that those instances will be present for its duration (at least until `test_list_instances` runs).  However, when two CI runs overlap, one can tear down / remove its temporary instance after the other has started, but before the `test_list_instances` run.

We can fix this one of two ways:

- Exclude CI-generated instances from `EXISTING_INSTANCES` (any matching the patterns `g-c-p-{unique_id}`, `new-{unique_id}` or `ndef-{unique_id}`).
- Update `test_list_instances` to assert only that the one instance known to be created (w/ `INSTANCE_ID`) is present.

If you look at the module documentation, `_work` is the target function -- like your `process_data_files`.  The function you want to use to configure the profiling is `pathos.profile.profiled` -- it's a decorator.  I don't use it as a decorator in the documented example, but you can.  The arguments of `profiled` configure how you want to run the profiler.  The decorator decorates a function, where the function you decorate will be profiled. 

So for `work = pr.profiled(**config)(_work)`, the `_work` function will be profiled.  However, for `_uimap = pr.profiled(**config)(pool.imap_unordered)`, the `imap_unordered` function will be profiled in the main thread or process, while whatever you pass in will get profiled will get profiled in their own thread or process.

The above is just to *configure* the profiling.  You have to turn on and off profiling with `enable_profiling` and `disable_profiling`.


There are two examples in the module documentation.  I was describing elements of the first example above, which uses "low-level" functions.  The following is the latter example, self-contained, which uses a "high-level" function.  Here `_test_import` is the target function with one argument (`'pox'` in the example), and `profile` is the decorator which prints the profiling table.

```
    >>> import pathos.profile as pr 
    >>>
    >>> # activate profiling
    >>> pr.enable_profiling()
    >>> 
    >>> # print stats for profile of 'import math' in another process
    >>> def test_import(module):
    ...    __import__(module)
    ...
    >>> import pathos.pools as pp
    >>> pool = pp.ProcessPool(1)
    >>> pr.profile('cumulative', pipe=pool.pipe)(test_import, 'pox')
         10 function calls in 0.003 seconds
   Ordered by: cumulative time
   ncalls  tottime  percall  cumtime  percall filename:lineno(function)
        1    0.000    0.000    0.003    0.003 <stdin>:1(test_import)
        1    0.002    0.002    0.003    0.003 {__import__}
        1    0.001    0.001    0.001    0.001 __init__.py:8(<module>)
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 shutils.py:11(<module>)
        1    0.000    0.000    0.000    0.000 _disk.py:15(<module>)

Could you post code that I could try and confirm that there are more processes created than requested?  Depending on how you created the `Pool`, this could be a bug or a feature.  I say this, as you have created the pool inside a class, which has the potential to spawn multiple `Pool` instances.

But, to answer your question. yes.  There are three levels of shutting down a `Pool`.  Two of them are the same as in `multiprocessing`, and one is unique to `pathos`.
```
>>> from pathos.pools import ProcessPool
>>> p = ProcessPool()
>>> p.map(lambda x:x, range(4))
[0, 1, 2, 3]
>>> # don't allow pool to take any more jobs
>>> p.close()
>>> # shut down all spawned processes
>>> p.join()
>>> # kill the pool and remove it from the pool cache
>>> p.terminate()
>>> # restore pool to a working state
>>> p.restart()
<multiprocess.pool.Pool object at 0x10cd9ead0>
>>> # shut down again...
>>> p.join()
>>> p.close()
>>> p.terminate()

It sounds like you are asking for either:
1. A way to give an existing role for deployment preferences
2. A way to define the resource name(s) within the generated policy (through a new policy template maybe?)
3. Maybe something similar to what we do for LambdaFunction and have a Policy section. This would allow you do add custom Policies to attach to the role but still allow SAM to generate the Role for you. 

How much control do you really want? Personally, I like the idea of the second way. This keeps the hand managing of resources down for customers. If we look at the AWS::Serverless::Function as an example, if you give a Role we do not append policies to that Role. This requires customers to now manage this Role and when they want to add DLQ support to their function, they need to remember to add this to the Role as well. Allowing SAM to generate these Roles for you as much as we can, will reduce this hand managing for customers. I think this makes for a better customer experience. The general flow is you write an acceptance test before any code. It should fail, of course, because the code that implements it isn't there. You mark the acceptance test with the @wip decorator so the test suite can skip these and therefore still pass. This acceptance test gets committed as a separate commit with message starting with 'acpt:...'.

Where the acceptance test fails determines what the first unit test and code is written. Each commit is atomic, generally meaning one unit test and just the code it takes to make it pass. After a few commits, the acceptance test passes, the @wip decorator is removed from it, and that's one cycle.

This pattern is clearly visible in the commit history, which you should study a bit to get the flow. The one for `python-pptx` has exactly the same pattern and is perhaps more active; just look for a commit starting with 'acpt:' and read up from there.  
https://github.com/scanny/python-pptx/commits/master

If you don't get this right you end up rewriting stuff or putting me to unnecessary effort (time) when it comes to merging.

Can't be fixed in the base layer, as the scripts / bin tools get dropped into a randomly create temp directory by pip during the install (i.e., the `--target` option doesn't work for scripts), and pip doesn't have an analogue to easy_install's `--script-dir`.

After some experimentation, the following gets both the libraries and bin tools under the target dir, as well as processing all the dependencies as it does currently:

    PYTHONUSERBASE=$target pip install --user --ignore-installed $spec

However, this ends up with more nesting in the target dir (`$target/bin`, and `$target/lib/pythonn2.7/site-packages/*`) which will have to be handled.

This change will allow `component.start()` users to control the output location and format of AB's logs as all AB loggers will be under the autobahn.* namespace.  AB does not call txaio's `start_logging()` using this method, so no output handlers or log format is set.

`run([components])` and `ApplicationRunner.run(ApplicationSession`) both call `start_logging()`. This attaches a handler to the root logger with a log format string which doesn't include the (now namespaced) logger name, so they will see no difference to the output format of AB's log messages.

However it will not fix the problems inherent to `start_logging()`'s use of the root logger e.g https://github.com/crossbario/autobahn-python/issues/952 - this would require some changes to the way `start_logging()` works, with any changes requiring this PR be in place first.

Coverage remained the same at 20.084% when pulling **58c37cfa5fffa89625e3c469199822dfe29ec3cf on shortdudey123:expand_elasticsearch_metrics** into **87ad08fe3bd47898c576e875f180487bd453561e on python-diamond:master**.

This collector reports two types of metric, values extracted from numeric fields on loggly events (in which case it does work just like a cron-like daemon) and event count during the collector interval which is more like the usual model.

You could argue that yes, this collector leans on diamond to run a cron-like task, the advantage over cron however is centralization. What we are trying to achieve with this collector is extract metrics off of log events and place them on some sort of metric aggregator system, e.g. graphite. It stands to reason that one would not use Diamond just for this collector but in addition to an array of others which is why it makes sense, in my opinion, to be able to use Diamond for all of them, instead of maintaining a separate cron job to retrieve log event metrics.

In short, the way I see it, from an abstract point of view, all collectors grab numeric values from some source and publish them to a given target, which is what this collector does as well. The event search is limited to the interval of the collector so the reported metrics are artificially made diamond-like.

From another angle, the driving force for this collector was a request from project management. The managers said in these words: "We have the duration of postgres long running queries in our logs on loggly, we want to see that as a timeseries on grafana" and that's the reason this collector exists.

Looks like a regression in the error message.  In 0.6.2 you get error message:

```
jmespath.exceptions.LexerError: Bad jmespath expression: Unknown character:
prefer-stable
      ^
```

Per the [spec](http://jmespath.org/specification.html#identifiers), identifiers are defined as:

```
identifier        = unquoted-string / quoted-string
unquoted-string   = (%x41-5A / %x61-7A / %x5F) *(  ; A-Za-z_
                        %x30-39  /  ; 0-9
                        %x41-5A /  ; A-Z
                        %x5F    /  ; _
                        %x61-7A)   ; a-z
```

so it needs to match `[A-Za-z_][0-9A-Z_a-z]*`.  For a key with a hyphen, you'll need to use a `quoted-string`, the expression should be:

```
>>> jmespath.search('"prefer-stable"', d)
```

Looks like the JMESPath tutorial actually has a bug that allows this character when it shouldn't.

That being said, I *think* we might be able to relax the constraint on unquoted identifiers to support this, but I'll need to do some digging to ensure this is an ok change.

In the meantime, you'll have to quote any key names that have chars beside `[A-Za-z0-9_]`.

Agreement in parameter styles discussion was that these exit codes should be flip-flopped, and mildly adjusted for unsuccessful task termination:
- if task terminates during waiting window, `exit(0)`
- if task does not terminate, `exit(1)`
- if task terminates with `status != SUCCESS`, `exit(1)`

As a future feature, we can map task statuses the way that `--map-http-status` is planned.

Hi @yaxxie! In principle, I am fine with any approach that allows turbodbc to be compatible with your use case while not adding any overhead to databases/drivers that behave appropriately. The one _big_ issue for me is that I would require a unit or integration test that the functionality actually works as intended. This test needs to be executed using the CI infrastructure, i.e., travis and/or AppVeyor. Also, things should be fixed for parameters if they require fixing.

I am not sure if the UTF16 thing really risks underallocating memory. Databases that support Unicode in `VARCHAR(n)` fields usually report `4*n` characters when pulling these values as `CHAR`. I would expect a database to report this as `2*n` characters when pulling these values as `WCHAR`. If the database internally uses UTF16 to store data (also supporting multicharacter sequences), the number of actual characters in a `VARCHAR(n)` field would depend on the number of multicharacter sequences in the specific value.

Actually, there's nothing I can do here at all.  I finally read the fine print.

You called this ticket, "allow use of date params in alias keys", but what you're asking for is for Curator to parse the `extra_settings`, which can be arbitrary.  Curator does not parse the `extra_settings` because it can't predict with any reliability what will be there.  It's just not a feasible feature request.

If you know what you're up against, you can always use environment variables and pass those to Curator.

Another thing I ran into was unescaped percent signs. I was using the command:

```
/usr/local/bin/curator delete indices --older-than 10 --time-unit days --timestring '%Y.%m.%d'
```

But I had to escape the percent signs, like so:

```
/usr/local/bin/curator delete indices --older-than 10 --time-unit days --timestring '\%Y.\%m.\%d'
```

Now my curator cron jobs run swimmingly!
absolutely, thank you!

Hi, @petitout 

The reason day of year isn't recognized is that it is not (yet) included as a viable choice.

See https://github.com/elastic/curator/blob/master/curator/api/filter.py#L16-L26

>```
DATE_REGEX = {
    'Y' : '4',
    'y' : '2',
    'm' : '2',
    'W' : '2',
    'U' : '2',
    'd' : '2',
    'H' : '2',
    'M' : '2',
    'S' : '2',
}
```

Day of year is a curious choice for index naming.  If you want to add it, you'll have to find a way to add it.  Does `%j` always send a 3-digit number _when used in python?_  If so, you could add a pull request to both add it to the `DATE_REGEX` constant, and write a test or two.

If you don't want to create a pull request, we can treat this issue as a feature request.  Just edit the title and add the label "enhancement."
It would result in a _ton_ of refactoring how the integration tests are called, but it'd be cleaner.

`:arg timeout:` is used when the `elasticsearch API accepts `timeout` as a parameter, it is an internal timeout of the operation and not related to the network timeout at all. For that use `request_timeout` or the global `timeout` (passed when constructing `Elasticsearch` instance - both of there parameters expect a number of seconds so `1ms` is not a valid value and should be `0.001` instead.

You can see the description here - https://elasticsearch-py.readthedocs.io/en/master/api.html#timeout

Hope this helps!

There is no special case for point versus general convex shape. I don't think using the current infrastructure is wasteful, since the choice was made to use a general purpose algorithm (GJK/EPA) to handle arbitrary convex shapes. Of course if you limit yourself to a few simple cases (sphere, capsule, cylinder, convex polyhedron) then you can optimize much better, and we probably should add some special code to perform point queries for those few special cases.

The GJK/EPA general algorithm can deal with much larger set of arbitrary convex shapes, including margins, Minkowski sums etc. Aside from closest point and penetration depth it also deals with continuous collision detection  / ray cast, convex shape cast under constant linear and angular velocity.

> the current OCA approach where merge = release is considered harmful

What you consider harmful I consider one of the best strengths of OCA.

Besides, OCA has stable branches: those with names like `8.0`, `9.0`, `10.0`, etc.; and it has development branches: [those with names like `pull/###/head`](https://help.github.com/articles/checking-out-pull-requests-locally/#modifying-an-inactive-pull-request-locally), I see no need for change there. You can checkout/merge/open PR to the one you want.

The **problem** here is that we have some guidelines on versioning, but they are not actually being followed because either devs are not forced to do so or they are not automated, thus there is no _reliable_ way for Odoo to know if an addon had an update.

The **solutions** AFAICS are:

1. Add a check in travis.
   - + Devs must go :white_check_mark: with bots, it's 1 LOC change, so not much harm.
   - - [Several PR for same addon at the same time would conflict](https://github.com/OCA/pylint-odoo/issues/59#issuecomment-254951868).
2. [Autobump last number (5th position, `fix`). Manual bump on others.
](https://github.com/OCA/pylint-odoo/issues/59#issuecomment-254972623)
   - + Most fixes will raise version automatically.
   - + Devs must still do manual update when a breaking change is included.
   - + Can be automated for Transifex commits too.
   - + No changes on guidelines.
3. [Autobump last number (6th position, `dev`). Manual bump on others.
](https://github.com/OCA/pylint-odoo/issues/59#issuecomment-254972623)
   - - Changes guidelines.
   - - Aims to change current workflow.
   - - Aims to merge unstable & stable code in the same branch.
   - + Most fixes will raise version automatically.
   - - Although that would have no benefit because additional manual work should be done by maintainers.
   - + Faster merges (although that's not a + for me...)
   - - Blurs the distinction between a `fix` and a `dev`. Seriously, what PR that is not a `breaking`, `improvement` nor `fix` could ever exist anywhere?
4. Forget this. `--update all` always.
   - + It works out of the box and right now.
   - - Quite a few downtime in big installations.
   - - Most downtime is spent updating things that are updated already.

My vote goes for option 2. Cast your votes please.

Please correct me if I forgot some option/pro/con.

--
As a side note, I'm thinking also on another approach for option 4, that could be also applied for option 2 if ever implemented:

0. Add a flag to warn your users about maintenance status.
0. Forbid updates for the currently connected Postgres user, restricting its permissions.
0. Duplicate the database.
0. Connect the outdated Odoo to the duplicate.
0. Restore write permissions in original DB.
0. Wake up an updated Odoo instance with `--update all` on the original database.
0. Shut down the outdated Odoo when the updated one finishes the update.
0. Wake up the updated one.
0. Destroy containers for old DB duplicate and old Odoo instance.

Yes, that would take a bigger amount of time, but it would be automatic and would mean close to zero downtime in the user's face. Well, they'd have some read-only time, but that's better than downtime.

Also, I think the commit with message "Add vspace support for cupy" might be better as "Add flatten support for cupy". However, it's using the old flatten code. Jamie's [new flatten code](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/7fa48ab4c5dfc87d0c2b4b88641ed42649f4a388/autograd/util.py#L93) dispatches to the vspace and would probably be simpler to use here. (Also I had another implementation of flatten in cupy/autograd_util.py for use with cupy, but I think Jamie's new version should make flatten work for anything that has vspaces set up.)

We have [the `solve_triangular` grad](https://github.com/HIPS/autograd/blob/master/autograd/scipy/linalg.py#L17-L28), so you can use that with `np.linalg.cholesky`. With that, you can write `cho_solve` in terms of two `solve_triangular` calls (the LAPACK routine [`dpotrs`](http://www.netlib.org/lapack/lapack-3.1.1/html/dpotrs.f.html) does the same thing, calling `dtrsm` twice).

Having certain values available only in certain hooks seems like a reactive anti-pattern to me. It shouldn't matter in which hook a handler runs. Could we maybe cache this info until the departed flag is actually cleared? That would make most sense to me, then it doesn't matter during which hook it runs.

Specifically, the outstanding questions as I understood them were:

* Is it ok that this somewhat reinforces the idea of being tied to the hook context?  (I seem to recall we were leaning toward this being ok for actions, as they are inherently different from hooks in that they are more on-demand.)
* This doesn't address the boillerplate necessary to load the reactive framework, as well as checking to ensure that the bootstrap has been run first.  Does that matter for this or should it just be handled as an additional PR?  How would you use the new decorator without it?
* Related to the previous, should the actions invoke the rest of the reactive bus?  Should it only be invoked if states are changed by the action?

Actually, looking at this more carefully, I realize that `after()` retrieves exactly one date, so the way to do what you want would be:

```python
    def dates_after(rr, basis_date, count=None):
    """
    Generator to generate `count` dates after basis_date from rrule rr

    :param rr:
        Instance of `dateutil.rrule` to iterate over.

    :param basis_date:
        The date from which to start generating dates (inclusive)

    :param count:
        The maximum number of dates to return - if None is passed this will yield dates until the
        rrule is exhausted.
    """
    # Needs to execute at least once
    cdate = rr.after(basis_date, inc=True)  # Include the basis date on the first run

    n = 0
    while cdate is not None:
        cdate = rr.after(cdate)

        yield cdate

        n += 1
        if cdate is None and count is not None and n >= count:
            raise StopIteration

    return None
```

I think the best thing to do is to add a `count` parameter to both `before()` and `after()` which, if specified, naturally returns a generator, but for now hoepfully the function above works for you.

Have just written a version of spatial softmax for a fixed number of dimensions.  It returns not only the argmax (averaged x, y, coordinates) but also the max (thinking this will give a hint as to the sharpness of the distribution).  Haven't run it yet, so, may need to update...but just dropping this here in case it helps, or in case you see an obvious error??

```
import numpy as np
import theano
import theano.tensor as T
import lasagne.layers as L
import lasagne.init as LI
import lasagne.nonlinearities as LN

class SpatialSoftmaxLayer(L.Layer):

    def __init__(self, incoming, temperature=1, learn_temperature=False,
            name='spatial_softmax'):
        super().__init__(incoming, name=name)
        if learn_temperature:
            self.temperature = self.add_param(LI.Constant(temperature), (1,),
                name="temperature", regularizable=False)
        else:
            self.temperature = theano.shared(np.array(temperature, dtype=floatX))

    def get_output_shape_for(self, input_shape):
        assert len(input_shape) == 4
        return input_shape[0], input_shape[1], 3

    def get_output_for(self, input, **kwargs):
        """ Performs 2D spatial softmax, returns expected x & y coordinates and
        the max value out of the softmax (to give a hint how sharp it is)"""
        assert len(input.shape) == 4
        n, c, h, w = input.shape
        input_2D = T.reshape(input, (n * c, h * w))
        softmax_2D = T.softmax(input_2D / self.temperature)
        softmax_4D = T.reshape(softmax_2D, (n, c, h, w))
        pos_h = theano.shared(np.linspace(-1., 1., num=h, dtype=floatX))
        pos_w = theano.shared(np.linspace(-1., 1., num=w, dtype=floatX))
        expected_h = T.dot(pos_h, softmax_4D).sum(axis=2)
        expected_w = T.dot(softmax_4D, pos_w).sum(axis=2)
        softmax_3D = T.reshape(softmax_2D, (n, c, h * w))
        z = T.max(softmax_3D, axis=2)
        output = T.stack([expected_h, expected_w, z], axis=2)
        return output
```

Also, an unrelated problem that still exists (just putting it here): when you don't put in the USB cable quickly enough, the device is recognized as a USB2 device. You have to pull it back out and put the cable back in (quickly and firmly enough) for it to be recognized as a USB3 device and enable all functionality.

Timeout waiting for frames is most often a symptom of an unsupported USB 3.0 host controller. We have also encountered plenty of systems where only 2 out of the 4 available physical USB 3.0 ports on a given machine will work! So, in order of priority:

  1. Only stream depth (remove extra bandwidth)
  2. Try a different (possibly thicker or shorter) USB 3.0 cable. Skip this step if you are using the one that came bundled with the R200.
  3. Try all the different 3.0 ports available to you
  4. Attempt to use a powered USB 3.0 hub (sometimes controllers don't negotiate enough power to the camera to actually stream data).
  5. Hop over to a Windows 8/10 machine to double check that the camera can stream at all

I know this is an old request at this point, but I was recently in need of this myself for something we're working on, and have a patch in progress that was going to be internal. Interested in it?

Basically, what it allows you to do is to define a `css_packages`/`js_packages` in your form or widget's `Media` and have that convert to either the bundled file (if `PIPELINE_ENABLED` is `True`) or the list of source files (if `PIPELINE_ENABLED` is `False`). Here's an example:

```python
from django import forms
from pipeline.forms import PipelineFormMedia

...

class MyForm(forms.Form):
    ...

    class Media(PipelineFormMedia):
        css_packages = {
            'all': ('my-form-styles', 'some-widget-styles'),
        }
        js_packages = ('my-form-scripts',)
```

Behind the scenes, `PipelineFormMedia` makes use of a metaclass that adds special `css` and `js` properties to the resulting class. When accessed by Django's form media support, these will go through the same rough process that the template tags go through, making a determination of whether to reference source or output files and then going through any necessary compilation process. This means automatic support for any custom widgets or forms when used in the Django admin UI.

The one difference between this and the template tags is that we're reliant on Django's hard-coded strings for doing the `<link>` or `<script>`, instead of the templates provided by Pipeline or the caller, but I think that's a reasonable sacrifice, in this case.

Interested in a patch?

For recipient status, you could probably do something like this:
* if total_accepted_recipients == total recipient count, use status "queued" for all recipients
* if total_rejected_recipients == total recipient count, use status "rejected" for all recipients
* in all other cases, use status "unknown" for all recipients -- because we can't known which ones were accepted and which rejected

(I think the most common case is a single recipient, so the "unknown" status should be pretty rare in practice.)

The primary point of the returned message_id is to match against later webhook status events on that message (and/or pass to status-checking APIs). So ideally, I think you'd want the `message_id` property that gets sent to [SparkPost's webhooks](https://support.sparkpost.com/customer/portal/articles/1976204-webhook-event-reference). But I think transmission_id would also work, as Anymail doesn't support batching multiple messages in the same API request (transmission). (It supports sending a *single* message to *multiple* recipients at once. If I'm understanding correctly, you'd treat that as a single transmission_id.)

Worst case, we could generate a true RFC Message-ID header (assuming SparkPost allows this header from the client), and use that as the unique message_id. That's how Anymail handles lack of a unique message_id [in the SendGrid backend](https://github.com/anymail/django-anymail/blob/v0.2/anymail/backends/sendgrid.py#L111-L122).

I basically subclassed django-anymail's `SendGridBackend` as such:

```python
class SendGridDynamicAuthenticationBackend(SendGridBackend):

    def __init__(self, **kwargs):
        """Init options from Django settings"""
        # Auth requires *either* SENDGRID_API_KEY or SENDGRID_USERNAME+SENDGRID_PASSWORD
        self.api_key = get_anymail_setting('SENDGRID_API_KEY', default=None, allow_bare=True)
        self.username = get_anymail_setting('SENDGRID_USERNAME', default=None, allow_bare=True)
        self.password = get_anymail_setting('SENDGRID_PASSWORD', default=None, allow_bare=True)

        if 'auth_username' in kwargs and 'auth_password' in kwargs:
            # This is custom to our needs.
            self.api_key = None
            self.username = kwargs.get('auth_username', None)
            self.password = kwargs.get('auth_password', None)

        if self.api_key is None and self.username is None and self.password is None:
            raise ImproperlyConfigured(
                "You must set either SENDGRID_API_KEY or both SENDGRID_USERNAME and "
                "SENDGRID_PASSWORD in your Django ANYMAIL settings."
                "Optionally you can set 'auth_username' & 'auth_password' "
                "kwargs via the get_connection method."
            )

        # This is SendGrid's Web API v2 (because the Web API v3 doesn't support sending)
        api_url = get_anymail_setting("SENDGRID_API_URL", "https://api.sendgrid.com/api/")
        if not api_url.endswith("/"):
            api_url += "/"

        # This is a hack and not an ideal way to do this.
        AnymailRequestsBackend.__init__(self, api_url, **kwargs)
```

I think @dean0x7d, @wjakob and I are in agreement that, at present, the maintenance burden of supporting MSVC 2015 update 2 or earlier is too high relative to the fairly small gains.

I think these two quotes (from #726), with which I'm in complete agreement, essentially sum it up:
@dean0x7d:
> Adding support for Update 2 now would require someone to install it locally (can't be installed side-by-side with Update 3), make the necessary workarounds and then hope it doesn't break in the future as there is no CI support.

@wjakob:
> Part of the motivation of this project is that it's okay to assume a reasonably correct compiler to avoid having to build complicated abstractions over semi-broken metaprogramming facilities.

If someone feels that support for earlier versions of compilers is sufficiently worthwhile and wants to commit to not only contribute the necessary workarounds for the current code **but also** to regularly dealing with future issues (because, quite frankly, I think most regular pybind11 contributors hate dealing with MSVC issues, and the older the release the more issues), I'd happily accept such a contribution.  But I don't want to deal with a semi-broken compiler, and I don't think it's fair to ask contributors to pybind11 to deal with potential issues; so until/unless such a contribution commitment is made, the list in this PR is going to be the minimum supported.

See the latest (d2b6719).  This disables implicit casting entirely if a new "implicit.h" header hasn't been included, keeping the .so size unchanged when code doesn't need it, while still providing the feature when desired.

> There's a reason why `pybind11/stl.h` isn't included automatically by `pybind11.h`, as it introduces possibly unwanted (and perhaps even arguably confusing) container conversion behavior.

The main reason is, I believe, just to keep the compilation a bit faster but not pulling in extra headers needed for stl containers in cases where they aren't needed.  Including doesn't exactly change the behaviour: without it, trying to return a stl container just results in a failure because pybind won't know how the cast the type to Python (because it'll get to the generic type caster, but `std::vector<Foo>` is not a registered type).  It's not clear to me that the non-`stl.h` behaviour is ever particular useful.

> I might be wrong, but I feel like `std::variant` and `std::optional` are in a bit of a different situation. 

I agree with you that `variant` and `optional` are essentially equivalent in their level of purpose to `tuple` and `pair`.  Those, however, are in `cast.h` sort of by accident: the function argument dispatcher used to use a `std::tuple`, with [special methods](https://github.com/pybind/pybind11/blob/v1.8/include/pybind11/cast.h#L647) in the tuple caster specific to loading argument lists.  That's no longer the case (as of 2.0, IIRC)—argument loading now goes via the `detail::argument_loader` class which is entirely separate.  So if `std::tuple` support were being added *today*, it might well be in `stl.h` (and `std::pair` goes along with `std::tuple`--as of 2.2 they are handled by the same code).

> I agree with the reorganization but there are diminishing returns. In particular, I believe that reorganizing api headers (those that enable different combinations of features) is way more important than reorganizing detail internals.

Agreed.  That's basically why we moved things into `detail/` for 2.2, but not other subdirectories.  Partly whether this happens depends on whether we do the caster overhaul for 2.3, which will benefit from it.

@nevion Regarding your updated example, this is currently doable without any reinterpret casts either:

```cpp
(const T*) arr.data(row, col)
```

Re: packed data, views and paddings: if an array object is C/F-contiguous (which it's not by default now but it will be, aligned + writeable + c_contiguous as default flag setting), strides are always evenly divisible by itemsize. Moreover, for `array_t<T>` (and later for `.astype<T>()`) it is required that `dtype.itemsize() == sizeof(T)`, in which case it really doesn't matter if T is packed or not (see test examples in `test_numpy_dtypes.cpp`).

So I assume the only edge case is presenting a misleading `T*` pointer to the user when the array is not c/f-contiguous (if they casted it to `T*` from `void*` the blame's on them), maybe it's a weird view that you don't want to copy but have to iterate over. But in this case any of these would work with `array_t` object, whatever the strides are, assuming the dtype is aligned:

```cpp
data_at(row, col) 
*data(row, col)
```

If you have an `array_t` and still want a `uint8_t*` because to be used in a tight loop where you don't want bounds checking etc, then yea, you will have to do a reinterpret cast, or a `(uint8_t*) arr.array::data()`. Idk, if it's such a big deal here, we could provide something like `uint8_t* array::bytes()`.

When you include any of the optional casters (`pybind11/eigen.h`, `pybind11/stl.h`, etc.), you need to include them consistently in every translation unit. The optional casters are specializations of `type_caster`, so if you don't include them everywhere `type_caster<EigenType>` will be a specialization in one translation unit, but fall back to `type_caster<T> : type_caster_generic` in another (where a header is missing). The ODR violation is happening because `type_caster<T>` now has different definitions for the same `T` in different translation units.

Oh, right, I was thinking `array` already had them.

Also: storing a `void*` would be better here with a cast to `T*` *after* computing the position (since numpy strides are in bytes, not element sizes).

I do not have a complete list of functionality the parser must have. In general, anything we are using `augeas` for in `certbot-apache` must be replaced either by another parser or additional functionality built around it. If you want to look through this yourself, usually the `augeas` object is referred to in the code as `aug`. Somewhat quickly looking the code, this functionality includes:

* Read in and represent an entire Apache configuration. This must be the entire configuration, not just virtual hosts.
* Get the value of any directive.
* Determine the file system path to the file containing a specific directive or object such as a virtual host.
* Add, modify, or delete directives at arbitrary places in a configuration. For example, being able to add the directive `SetHandler server-status` to a specific location block, vhost, and file of an Apache config. This must work for both single line directives and block directives like `Location`.
* Search a configuration for all directives of a specific type such as `VirtualHosts`.
* Copy directives (including block directives) from one place in the the configuration to another.
* Create new files and add them to the Apache configuration.
* Be able to make changes in memory and later write out all modifications to the user's Apache config.
* Preserve all whitespace, formatting, comments, etc. Reading a configuration in and immediately writing it back out should not change the contents of a user's file in any way.

Triggering rerun via `rm -f` is a convention of `Make` as well as possibly a number of workflow systems. So the "Pros" here is apparent. Another important argument is that I think in practice there are more user cases that we want to remove unwanted files (due to experiment failure) and trigger rerun -- this is the "research mode". Removing intermediate large files without triggering rerun often happens when a pipeline is somewhat mature and the product reliable -- this is the "production mode". I cannot speak for others, but at least I find myself in research mode a lot because I just couldnt get things work most of the time ... my office mate also expects this behavior. Therefore Solution 2 is most intuitive for us.

The problem here is that the `task` files are usually on remote host. What we do right now is

1. transfer `.task` file to remote host
2. create `.sh` file and send to remote host
3. submit the job and obtain `job_id`

If we are to add `job_id` to task, we will have to modify the task file locally and re-send `.task` file to remote host, which can be done but does not look like a good idea.

i.e.
```
def detn_l1(input, target):
    bb_t,_ = target
    bb_i = input[:, :4]
    bb_i = F.sigmoid(bb_i)*224
    return F.l1_loss(V(bb_i),V(bb_t)).data

def detn_acc(input, target):
    _,c_t = target
    c_i = input[:, 4:]
    return accuracy(c_i, c_t)
```
